summary:
East Timor (Timor-Leste) voted for independence from Indonesia in a UN-supervised referendum on August 30, 1999, with 78.5 percent of voters supporting independence over special autonomy. Indonesia had invaded and occupied East Timor in 1975, shortly after Portugal ended its colonial rule, killing an estimated 100,000 to 180,000 people — approximately a quarter of the population — through military operations, famine, and disease during the subsequent occupation. Indonesia's annexation was never recognized by the United Nations.

Following the referendum vote, pro-Indonesian militias — backed by elements of the Indonesian military — launched a systematic campaign of violence and destruction across East Timor, killing hundreds, displacing 400,000 people, and destroying much of the territory's infrastructure. International outrage and pressure on the Indonesian government, combined with the political transition underway following the [[event:3690|fall of Suharto]] in 1998, resulted in Indonesia agreeing to allow an Australian-led international peacekeeping force (INTERFET) to restore order.

East Timor became a United Nations Trust Territory and formally achieved independence as the Democratic Republic of Timor-Leste on May 20, 2002, becoming the twenty-first century's first new nation. The story of East Timorese independence illustrated both the possibilities and limits of international intervention to protect self-determination rights, and the long-term consequences of Cold War-era Western acquiescence to Indonesia's brutal occupation.

read_more_links.title: Indonesian occupation of East Timor - Wikipedia
